term: Servant of Jehovah; Servant of the Lord; Servant of Yahweh
intro:
The Servant of Yahweh (Hebrew eved YHWH) is the figure who appears in four 'Servant Songs' within Isaiah 40-55: 42:1-4, 49:1-6, 50:4-9, and 52:13-53:12. The Servant is identified within Isaiah both with Israel as a corporate entity ('you are my servant, Israel, in whom I will display my splendor' — 49:3) and as an individual who brings Israel back from exile and is 'a light for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth' (49:6) — suggesting a representative individual who fulfills and embodies Israel's calling. The Fourth Song (52:13-53:12) describes the Servant's vicarious suffering in detail: 'he was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed' (53:5).

The NT applies the Servant Songs to Jesus more extensively than any other OT passage. Matthew 12:17-21 quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 in connection with Jesus's healing ministry. John 12:38 cites Isaiah 53:1 explaining Israel's unbelief. Acts 8:32-35 records Philip explaining Isaiah 53:7-8 to the Ethiopian eunuch as fulfilled in Jesus. Romans 4:25 echoes Isaiah 53:12 ('he was raised to life for our justification'). The identification of the Servant with Jesus was complete within the first generation of Christian proclamation and represents one of the deepest typological connections between the Testaments. Jesus's own statement that he came 'to give his life as a ransom for many' (Mark 10:45; cf. Isa. 53:11-12) anchors the Servant identification in his self-understanding.
